methods.description: This method describes the process for analyzing the concentration of Cu, Zn, and Pb in natural seawater and porewater using ICP-MS, and the subsequent preparation of enriched seawater for culturing experiments by adding water-soluble salts. It also covers the setup of control groups and salinity adjustment.

steps.description: The concentration of Cu, Zn and Pb in natural seawater and porewater (3 replicates) at the sampling site was analyzed using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, -Agilent IPC-MS 7900 quadrupole mass spectrometry system, quality control reference material: US Canadian certified reference material: Lake Sediment LKSD-1).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure metal concentrations (Cu, Zn, Pb) in natural seawater and porewater samples using ICP-MS.
outputs: Concentrations of Cu, Zn, and Pb in natural seawater and porewater

steps.description: For the ICP-MS measurements of the water samples, immediately after sampling 1 μl HCl (36%) were added per ml natural seawater or porewater.
steps.searchable_summary: Acidify water samples with HCl for ICP-MS analysis.

steps.description: Water-soluble salts (CuSO 4 *5 H 2 0, ZnSO 4 *7 H 2 O, PbCl 2 ) were used to enrich these metals in natural seawater used for culturing experiments.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare enriched seawater by adding water-soluble metal salts.

steps.description: Sea water was amended with heavy elements at different levels and heavy elements measured using ICP-MS before starting the experiment (a list of the measured concentrations of Cu, Zn and Pb in amended seawater is given in Table 1). A wide range of Zn concentration was investigated by increasing the Zn concentrations up to 1000-fold from natural seawater Cu should be lower enriched (5 and 25-fold) because this element is probably known as toxic metal for many organisms. A higher Cu enrichment (100-fold) was planned, but not validated in the following manuscript due to Cu precipitation. Following the low Pb concentration in the sea water a wide range from 2.5 up to 500-fold enrichment was used to test the influence of the foraminiferal behavior. The exact enrichments are given in Table 1, which differ slightly from the calculated ones, because the used salts were hydrophile, which made them more difficult to handle.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare seawater samples with varying target enrichment levels of Cu, Zn, and Pb.
inputs: Target enrichment levels (e.g., Zn up to 1000x, Cu 5x/25x, Pb 2.5x-500x)
outputs: Seawater samples with specific target metal enrichment levels

steps.description: heavy elements measured using ICP-MS before starting the experiment (a list of the measured concentrations of Cu, Zn and Pb in amended seawater is given in Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Verify measured metal concentrations in prepared amended seawater using ICP-MS.
outputs: Measured concentrations of Cu, Zn, and Pb in amended seawater (Table 1)

steps.description: Seawater without additional salts (Zn 0, Cu 0 and Pb 0) was used as a control to obtain the food uptake of foraminifera under natural environmental conditions.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare control seawater sample without added metal salts.

steps.description: For the other experiments only one heavy element was enriched, the other two have still the same concentration like the natural seawater (Table 1). So, a clear response of the foraminifera attributed to one added metal dose can be observed.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare seawater samples with single metal enrichment.
inputs: Metal salts (CuSO4*5 H2O, ZnSO4*7 H2O, PbCl2)
outputs: Seawater samples enriched with only one metal

steps.description: To avoid a salinity impact of the treatment, the salinity of the culture medium was measured after adding heavy element salts...
steps.searchable_summary: Measure salinity of the prepared culture medium.

steps.description: ...and was corrected, if necessary, with distilled water.
steps.searchable_summary: Adjust salinity of culture medium using distilled water if needed.

methods.method_name: Foraminifera Sample Preparation for Isotope Analysis
methods.description: This method details the steps for preparing foraminifera samples for subsequent carbon and nitrogen isotope ratio measurements, including transfer to capsules, drying, decalcification, and final drying and storage.

steps.description: For element and isotope measurements, foraminifera were transferred into tin capsules (Sn 99.9, IVA Analysentechnik GmbH & Co. KG)...
steps.searchable_summary: Transfer foraminifera samples into tin capsules.

steps.description: ...and air dried for 3 days.
steps.searchable_summary: Air dry foraminifera samples in tin capsules for 3 days.
outputs: Air-dried foraminifera samples in tin capsules

steps.description: The natural abundance of the isotopes (blanks) was measured from foraminifera (triplicates which each 20 individuals) which were taken from the main culture and are not further cultivated in any other solutions.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure baseline isotope ratios (blanks) in control foraminifera samples.

steps.description: Foraminiferal tests were then decalcified by adding 5 μl 4% hydrochloric acid twice.
steps.searchable_summary: Decalcify foraminifera samples using hydrochloric acid.

steps.description: The dissolution took place in a drying cabinet at 60 C.
steps.searchable_summary: Dissolve decalcified samples by heating in a drying cabinet.

steps.description: As a final step, the samples were left in the drying cabinet at 60 C for another 24 h to remove any residual moisture.
steps.searchable_summary: Dry dissolved samples at 60 C for 24 hours to remove moisture.

steps.description: The samples were weighed to the nearest μg...
steps.searchable_summary: Weigh prepared foraminifera samples.

steps.description: ...and kept in the desiccator until isotope ratio measurement.
steps.searchable_summary: Store prepared samples in a desiccator.

methods.description: This method describes the process of measuring the ratios of 13C/12C and 15N/14N in prepared foraminifera samples using an Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometer (IRMS) coupled to an elemental analyzer, utilizing specified isotope standards.

steps.description: The ratios of 13 C/ 12 C and 15 N/ 14 N were determined using an isotope ratio mass spectrometer (IRMS, Delta PLUS , coupled by a ConFlo III interface to an elemental analyzer, EA 1110, Thermo Finnigan).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure carbon and nitrogen isotope ratios (13C/12C, 15N/14N) using EA-IRMS.
inputs: Prepared foraminifera samples (from Foraminifera Sample Preparation method)
EA-IRMS system (Delta PLUS IRMS, ConFlo III interface, EA 1110)

steps.description: Vienna PeeDee Belemnite (R VPDB ¼ 0.0112372) and atmospheric dinitrogen (R atmN ¼ 0.0036765) were used as the isotope standards for C and N.
steps.searchable_summary: Use VPDB and atmospheric dinitrogen as standards for C and N isotope ratio calibration.

methods.description: This method outlines the statistical procedures used to evaluate the effects of heavy element level and time on experimental outcomes, specifically using multifactorial ANOVA and multiple range tests with a defined significance level.

steps.description: For statistical evaluation of the effects of heavy element level and time multifactorial ANOVA... was carried out using StatGraphics Centurion 18...
steps.searchable_summary: Perform multifactorial ANOVA on experimental data.
inputs: Experimental data (e.g., foraminiferal response, heavy element levels, time)

steps.description: ...and multiple range test was carried out using StatGraphics Centurion 18...
steps.searchable_summary: Perform multiple range test on experimental data.
outputs: Multiple range test results (e.g., group comparisons, significance groupings)

steps.description: ...(significant cut of: α ¼ 0.05).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply a statistical significance level of alpha = 0.05.
inputs: Statistical test results (ANOVA, multiple range test)
